title: Paycheck Fairness Act
plain_summary: The Paycheck Fairness Act is a law that helps make sure men and women get paid the same amount for doing the same job. It makes it harder for companies to have unfair reasons for paying people differently and protects workers who want to talk about how much money they make. Companies that don't follow the rules will face bigger punishments, and they have to report information about how much they pay workers of different genders and races. The law also stops employers from asking job candidates about their past salaries and creates programs to teach people about fair pay.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Paycheck Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. Women have joined the workforce in record numbers over the past fifty years. Even though the Equal Pay Act was passed in nineteen sixty-three, many women still earn much less money than men for doing the same work. This happens in both private companies and government jobs. The pay gaps are especially bad for women and girls of color. In many cases, these pay differences can only be caused by discrimination that is still happening or the effects of past discrimination. Even when researchers control for education level, job type, industry, union membership, race, ethnicity, and work experience, about forty percent of the pay gap cannot be explained. These pay differences hurt working families who need all family members' wages to get by. They hurt women's retirement security, which depends on earnings during their working years. They prevent women from reaching their full economic potential in terms of joining and staying in the workforce. These problems have spread through business and commerce among workers in different states. They burden business and the free flow of goods. They create unfair competition in business. They cause workplace disputes, as shown by tens of thousands of complaints filed with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission between two thousand ten and two thousand sixteen. They interfere with fair marketing of goods. In many cases, they may deny workers equal protection based on sex, violating the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution. Artificial barriers to stopping sex-based pay discrimination still exist decades after the Fair Labor Standards Act of nineteen thirty-eight and the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty-four were passed. These barriers exist mainly because the Equal Pay Act of nineteen sixty-three has not worked as Congress originally intended. Improvements and changes to the law are needed to make sure the Act provides effective protection to people facing pay discrimination based on their sex. Removing these barriers would have positive effects, including solving problems in the economy caused by unfair pay differences, greatly reducing the number of working women earning unfairly low wages and reducing dependence on public assistance, promoting stable families by letting all family members earn fair pay, fixing the effects of past sex-based discrimination and ensuring future workers get equal protection based on sex, and ensuring equal protection under Congress's power to enforce the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution. The Department of Labor and the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission work to help ensure that women get equal pay for equal work. The Department of Labor is responsible for collecting and making public information about women's pay, sharing information about women's workplace rights, helping women who have been victims of pay discrimination get help, and investigating and prosecuting widespread gender-based pay discrimination involving government contractors. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is the main enforcement agency for claims made under the Equal Pay Act of nineteen sixty-three, and issues rules and guidance on proper interpretations of the law. Strong action by the Department of Labor and the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, increased information from the changes made by this Act, wage data, and more effective remedies will help women better recognize and enforce their rights. Some employers have already made great progress in removing unfair pay differences in the workplace and their achievements should be recognized.

sections.title: Section 3  Enhanced Enforcement of Equal Pay Requirements
sections.text: This section changes the Fair Labor Standards Act to make equal pay enforcement stronger. The law now requires employers who want to pay men and women differently to prove that the reason is a legitimate factor other than sex, such as education, training, or experience. To use this defense, employers must show that the factor is not based on sex differences in pay, is related to the job, is necessary for business, and explains the entire pay difference. This defense does not work if the employee shows that there is another way to meet the same business need without creating pay differences and the employer refused to use that other way. Employees are considered to work in the same workplace if they work for the same employer in locations within the same county or similar local government area. This does not limit broader uses of the term workplace as defined by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission. The law now protects employees from retaliation if they have filed complaints, participated in investigations or hearings related to this Act, or have asked about, discussed, or shared information about their own wages or another employee's wages, such as asking an employer why wages are set at a certain rate. Employers cannot require employees to sign contracts that would stop them from sharing information about their wages. However, employees who have access to other employees' wage information as part of their essential job duties cannot share other employees' wages with people who do not normally have access to that information, unless the sharing is in response to a complaint or to help with an investigation or hearing under the equal pay law. The penalties for violating equal pay laws are now stronger. Employers who violate the equal pay law must pay additional money to make up for harm caused to employees, or if the employee shows the employer acted with malice or reckless disregard, punitive damages as appropriate. The United States government cannot be required to pay punitive damages. Class action lawsuits can now be filed to enforce the equal pay law under Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Courts can award attorney fees including expert fees in these cases. When the Secretary of Labor takes action against employers, they can seek additional compensatory or punitive damages as described above. Class action cases can begin when an individual becomes a party to the class action lawsuit.

sections.title: Section 4  Training
sections.text: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission and the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs must provide training to their employees and affected people and organizations on issues involving discrimination in wage payment, if Congress provides funding under Section eleven.

sections.title: Section 5  Negotiation Skills Training
sections.text: The Secretary of Labor, after talking with the Secretary of Education, can create and run a grant program. The Secretary of Labor can give competitive grants to eligible organizations to run negotiation skills training programs to address pay differences, including outreach to women and girls. Eligible organizations include public agencies like states, local governments in metropolitan areas, state education agencies, local education agencies, private nonprofit organizations, or community-based organizations. To get a grant, an organization must submit an application to the Secretary of Labor at the required time, in the required way, with the required information. Organizations that get grants must use the money to run effective negotiation skills training programs for the purposes described. The Secretary of Labor and Secretary of Education must issue rules or policy guidance to include negotiation skills training, as much as possible, into existing programs. For the Secretary of Education, this includes programs under the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of nineteen sixty-five, the Carl D. Perkins Career and Technical Education Act of two thousand six, the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five, and other Department of Education programs that the Secretary determines are appropriate. For the Secretary of Labor, this includes programs under the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act and other Department of Labor programs that the Secretary determines are appropriate. No later than eighteen months after this Act becomes law, and every year after that, the Secretary of Labor, working with the Secretary of Education, must prepare and submit to Congress a report describing the activities done under this section and evaluating how effective these activities are in achieving the purposes of this section.

sections.title: Section 6  Research, Education, and Outreach
sections.text: No later than eighteen months after this Act becomes law, and regularly after that, the Secretary of Labor must conduct studies and provide information to employers, labor organizations, and the public about ways to eliminate pay differences between men and women, including women who are Asian American, Black or African American, Hispanic American or Latino, Native American or Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, and White American. This includes conducting and promoting research to develop ways to quickly fix conditions leading to pay differences, with special attention to women and girls from historically underrepresented and minority groups. The Secretary must publish and make available to employers, labor organizations, professional associations, educational institutions, the media, and the public the findings from studies and other materials about eliminating pay differences. The Secretary must sponsor and help state, local, and community information and education programs. The Secretary must provide information to employers, labor organizations, professional associations, and other interested people on ways to eliminate pay differences. The Secretary must recognize and promote achievements of employers, labor organizations, and professional associations that have worked to eliminate pay differences. No later than one year after this Act becomes law, the Secretary of Labor, working through the Director of the Women's Bureau and coordinating with the Commissioner of Labor Statistics, must submit to Congress a report on the gender pay gap in the teenage workforce and make the report available on a public Department of Labor website. The report must include an examination of trends and potential solutions for the teenage gender pay gap, how the teenage gender pay gap might lead to bigger wage gaps in the overall workforce, overall lifetime earnings and losses for informal and formal jobs for women including women of color, the teenage gender pay gap including a comparison of average earnings by males and females in informal jobs like babysitting and other freelance jobs as well as formal jobs like retail, restaurant, and customer service. The report must compare the types of tasks typically done by women from teenage years through adulthood in certain informal and formal jobs with the types of tasks done by younger males in such positions. The report must include interviews and surveys with workers and employers about early gender-based pay differences. The report must include recommendations for addressing pay inequality for women from teenage years through adulthood including women of color, addressing any disadvantages experienced by young women regarding work experience and professional development, developing standards and best practices for workers and employees to ensure better pay for young women and prevent early workplace inequalities, and expanding awareness for teenage girls about pay rates and employment rights to reduce greater inequalities in the overall workforce.

sections.title: Section 7  Establishment of the National Award for Pay Equity in the Workplace
sections.text: This section creates the Secretary of Labor's National Award for Pay Equity in the Workplace, which will be given every year to an employer to encourage active efforts to follow the equal pay law as changed by this Act. The Secretary of Labor will set standards for getting the award, including a requirement that an employer has made substantial effort to eliminate pay differences between men and women and deserves special recognition for such effort. The Secretary will establish procedures for applying for and presenting the award. In this section, employer includes a corporation including a nonprofit corporation, a partnership, a professional association, a labor organization, and a business entity similar to these. It also includes an entity running an education referral program, a training program such as an apprenticeship or management training program, or a similar program. It also includes an entity running a joint program formed by a combination of any of these entities.

sections.title: Section 8  Collection of Pay Information by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission
sections.text: This section adds new requirements to the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty-four. No later than eighteen months after this section becomes law, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission must collect from employers compensation data and other employment-related data including hiring, termination, and promotion data broken down by sex, race, and ethnic identity of employees. In doing this, the Commission must primarily consider the most effective and efficient ways to enhance enforcement of federal laws prohibiting pay discrimination. The Commission must consider factors including the burden on employers, how often reports are required including the size of employers required to prepare reports, appropriate protections for keeping data confidential, and the most effective format to report such data. For each twelve-month reporting period for an employer, the compensation data collected must include, for each range of taxable compensation broken down by categories the Commission determines, the number of employees who earn taxable compensation in each range and the total number of hours worked by such employees. The Commission sets specific dollar ranges for taxable compensation, starting from not more than nineteen thousand two hundred thirty-nine dollars up to not less than two hundred eight thousand dollars. The Commission can adjust these ranges if needed to enhance enforcement of federal laws prohibiting pay discrimination or for inflation, working with the Bureau of Labor Statistics. For employees who are not required to get overtime pay, an employer can report that full-time employees work forty hours per week and part-time employees work twenty hours per week, or report actual hours worked. The categories must include race, ethnic identity, sex, and job categories including those described in the Equal Employment Opportunity Employer Information Report EEO-1 instructions as they existed when this section was enacted. The Commission must use the compensation data collected to enhance investigation of charges filed under equal pay laws and allocation of resources to investigate such charges, and for any other purpose the Commission determines appropriate. The Commission must annually make publicly available aggregate compensation data collected for these categories, broken down by industry, occupation, and core based statistical area as defined by the Office of Management and Budget. The compensation data must be collected from each employer that is a private employer with one hundred or more employees, including employers that are federal government contractors or subcontractors at any level, or other employers the Commission determines appropriate.

sections.title: Section 9  Reinstatement of Pay Equity Programs and Pay Equity Data Collection
sections.text: The Commissioner of Labor Statistics must continue to collect data on women workers in the Current Employment Statistics survey. The Director of the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs must ensure that office employees use the full range of investigatory tools available including pay grade methodology. When considering evidence of possible compensation discrimination, they cannot limit consideration to a small number of types of evidence or limit evaluation to a small number of methods, and cannot require a multiple regression analysis or anecdotal evidence for a compensation discrimination case. For investigative, compliance, and enforcement activities, they must define similarly situated employees in a way that is consistent with and not more strict than the definition in the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission Compliance Manual from two thousand, and must consider only factors that investigation reveals were used in making compensation decisions. They must implement a survey to collect compensation data and other employment-related data including hiring, termination, and promotion data and designate at least half of all nonconstruction contractor establishments each year to prepare and file such survey, and must review and use the responses to identify contractor establishments for further evaluation and for other enforcement purposes as appropriate. The Secretary of Labor must make readily available accurate information on compensation discrimination, including statistics, explanations of employee rights, historical analyses of such discrimination, instructions for employers on compliance, and any other information that will help the public understand and address such discrimination. This information must be available in print, on the Department of Labor website, and through any other forum that the Department uses to distribute compensation discrimination information.

sections.title: Section 10  Prohibitions Relating to Prospective Employees' Salary and Benefit History
sections.text: This section adds a new section to the Fair Labor Standards Act of nineteen thirty-eight. It will be illegal for an employer to rely on the wage history of a potential employee when considering them for employment in positions covered by federal wage laws, including requiring that a potential employee's prior wages meet minimum or maximum criteria as a condition of being considered for employment. Employers cannot rely on wage history when determining wages for potential employees for such positions, except that an employer can rely on wage history if it is voluntarily provided by a potential employee after the employer makes a job offer with compensation to support a wage higher than offered by the employer. Employers cannot seek wage history from a potential employee or any current or former employer of the potential employee for such positions, except that an employer can seek to confirm prior wage information only after making a job offer with compensation and the potential employee responds by providing prior wage information to support a wage higher than offered by the employer. Employers cannot fire or retaliate against any employee or potential employee for such positions because they opposed any act made illegal by this section or took an action for which discrimination is forbidden under existing retaliation protections. In this section, wage history means the wages paid to the potential employee by their current employer or previous employer. Any person who violates these provisions will be subject to a civil penalty of five thousand dollars for a first offense, increased by an additional one thousand dollars for each subsequent offense, not to exceed ten thousand dollars. They will also be liable to each employee or potential employee who was the subject of the violation for special damages not to exceed ten thousand dollars plus attorney fees, and will be subject to appropriate injunctive relief. An action to recover this liability can be maintained against any employer including a public agency in any federal or state court by any one or more employees or potential employees for themselves and other employees or potential employees similarly situated.

sections.title: Section 11  Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress authorizes the appropriation of such sums as may be necessary to carry out this Act. None of the funds appropriated for purposes of the grant program in Section five of this Act may be used for a congressional earmark as defined in clause nine (e) of rule twenty-one of the Rules of the House of Representatives.

sections.title: Section 12  Small Business Assistance
sections.text: This Act and the changes made by this Act will take effect on the date that is six months after the date this Act is signed into law. The Secretary of Labor and the Commissioner of the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission must jointly develop technical assistance material to help small enterprises comply with the requirements of this Act and the changes made by this Act. A small enterprise will be exempt from the provisions of this Act and the changes made by this Act to the same extent that such enterprise is exempt from the requirements of the Fair Labor Standards Act of nineteen thirty-eight under specific clauses of that Act.

sections.title: Section 13  Rule of Construction
sections.text: Nothing in this Act or in any changes made by this Act will affect the obligation of employers and employees to fully comply with all applicable immigration laws, including being subject to any penalties, fines, or other sanctions.

sections.title: Section 14  Severability
sections.text: If any provision of this Act, a change made by this Act, or the application of that provision or change to particular persons or circumstances is held invalid or found to be unconstitutional, the remainder of this Act, the changes made by this Act, or the application of that provision to other persons or circumstances will not be affected.
